name: causal_reasoning
description: Tests multi-hop causal chain traversal. Memories form cause-effect chains that require following edges to answer correctly.

conversation_history.content: I started learning Python because my manager suggested it for automating reports.
I found a Python course on Udemy that covers pandas for data analysis.
Had a really nice lunch at the new Thai place across the street. The pad see ew was excellent.
It was raining all day today, couldn't even go for my usual walk after lunch.
Sat in on a meeting about the new office seating arrangement. Apparently we're switching to hot desks next month.
The pandas course taught me about DataFrames which I used to parse our sales CSV files.
Parsing the sales CSV revealed that Q3 revenue dropped 15% compared to Q2.
Dave from marketing keeps talking about his fantasy football team. Apparently he's in three leagues this year.
The coffee machine on the 3rd floor broke again. Had to go down to the lobby for my afternoon espresso.
Our design team is working on a brand refresh. They showed some new logo concepts at the all-hands.
I need to remember to pick up groceries on the way home — we're out of eggs and milk.
The Q3 revenue drop was caused by losing the Acme Corp contract worth $200K.
We lost the Acme Corp contract because our delivery times were consistently late.
Beautiful weather today, sunny and about 72 degrees. Perfect fall day.
Had a one-on-one with Sarah from HR about our annual benefits enrollment. Dental plan options are confusing.
Went to a team trivia night after work. Our team came in second place — lost by one question.
There's a new project management tool the company wants us to trial — some Jira alternative called Linear.
The late deliveries were traced back to a warehouse staffing shortage in July.
I presented the full root cause analysis to my manager using a Python-generated report.
Tried making sourdough bread over the weekend. Total disaster — the starter wasn't active enough.
My car needs an oil change. Should probably schedule that for Saturday morning.
The IT department rolled out a new VPN client. It's slower than the old one but supposedly more secure.
Had a brainstorm session with the product team about Q1 feature priorities. Lots of ideas but no decisions yet.
Based on my analysis, the company hired 5 new warehouse staff to prevent future delays.
My manager promoted me to data analyst role because of the impact of my Python work.
Saw a great documentary about octopuses last night. They can solve puzzles and open jars.
Went to the gym this morning before work. Finally hit a new personal record on deadlifts.
The office plants are looking rough. Someone suggested we get a plant care service.
In my new role I'm building a real-time dashboard to monitor delivery performance.
Lisa from accounting mentioned they're switching to a new invoicing system next quarter.
Weekend plans: going hiking at the state park if the weather holds up.
The building elevator was out of service this morning. Had to take the stairs to the 7th floor.
The dashboard caught a new bottleneck in the packaging department before it caused delays.
My neighbor got a new puppy, a golden retriever. Very cute but it barks constantly.
Tom from engineering asked if I could pull some API latency data for their performance review.
Finished binge-watching that sci-fi show everyone's been recommending. The ending was disappointing.
We had a fire drill today. Stood outside for 25 minutes in the cold.
The marketing team is planning a customer appreciation event for next month. They asked analytics to help with the invite list segmentation.
Traffic was terrible this morning. A fender bender on the highway added 30 minutes to my commute.
Had sushi for lunch with a few coworkers. The new place on 5th has surprisingly good omakase.
The facilities team replaced all the fluorescent lights with LEDs. The office looks completely different now.
Joined a cross-functional committee on improving internal communications. First meeting was mostly introductions.
Started reading a book on data visualization best practices for my dashboard work.
It snowed last night. First snow of the season and it made driving into work a nightmare.
The quarterly company town hall happened today. CEO seems optimistic about next year's targets.
My standing desk motor broke. IT said they'll replace it but the wait is two weeks.
Attended a webinar on data pipeline architecture. Might be useful for scaling the analytics platform later.
Coworker brought in homemade cookies today. Chocolate chip with sea salt — genuinely amazing.
The sales team asked me to help build a forecast model for Q1. Seems like a good way to expand my impact.
Realized I forgot to water my desk plant for two weeks. It's barely hanging on.
Had a skip-level meeting with the VP of operations. She asked about the dashboard metrics and seemed impressed.
Tried a new coffee shop near the office. Their cold brew is way too strong — nearly vibrated out of my chair.
The parking garage is doing maintenance so we have to park on the street for the next two weeks.
Our team offsite is coming up. They booked a bowling alley which should be fun.
Spent the afternoon debugging a data pipeline issue. Turned out to be a timezone conversion bug.
It's been unseasonably warm this week. People are eating lunch outside on the patio.
The legal department sent out the annual compliance training reminder. Due by end of month.
Helped the new junior analyst get set up with Python and walked her through the pandas basics.
My gym buddy moved to another city. Need to find a new workout partner.
Overheard someone in the break room complaining about the vending machine eating their dollar again.
The marketing team launched a big social media campaign targeting enterprise customers.
Marketing's enterprise campaign generated 40 new inbound leads in the first month.
The new leads from the marketing campaign converted into $150K in new contracts.
The $150K in new contracts partially offset the Acme Corp loss, but Q4 revenue is still below target.
Someone left a half-eaten sandwich in the fridge for a week. The whole break room smells terrible.
Had a long debate with coworkers about the best pizza toppings. Pineapple was controversial as always.
The office printer jammed three times today. IT says they're ordering a replacement.
Someone accidentally set off the smoke alarm by microwaving popcorn too long.
The elevator music changed from jazz to classical. Mixed reactions from the team.
Tried to fix my standing desk wobble with a piece of cardboard. Surprisingly effective.

questions.question: What was the root cause chain that led to the Q3 revenue drop?
questions.ground_truth: Warehouse staffing shortage in July caused late deliveries, which caused the loss of the Acme Corp contract ($200K), which caused the Q3 revenue drop of 15%.

questions.question: How did learning Python ultimately lead to a promotion?
questions.ground_truth: Manager suggested Python -> took Udemy pandas course -> parsed sales CSVs -> discovered Q3 revenue drop -> did root cause analysis -> presented report -> got promoted to data analyst.

questions.question: What concrete business action resulted from the data analysis?
questions.ground_truth: The company hired 5 new warehouse staff to prevent future delivery delays, based on the root cause analysis that traced revenue loss to warehouse staffing shortage.

questions.question: What was the original trigger that eventually led to hiring 5 warehouse staff?
questions.ground_truth: The manager suggested learning Python -> user took pandas course -> parsed sales CSVs -> discovered Q3 revenue drop -> traced to lost Acme contract -> traced to late deliveries -> traced to warehouse staffing shortage -> company hired 5 new staff. The original trigger was the manager's suggestion to learn Python.

questions.question: What is the connection between the Python course and the dashboard?
questions.ground_truth: Python course taught pandas/DataFrames -> used for sales analysis -> led to promotion to data analyst -> now building real-time delivery dashboard in new role.

questions.question: There were two separate revenue impacts — one negative and one positive. What caused each and how are they related?
questions.ground_truth: Negative: warehouse staffing shortage -> late deliveries -> lost Acme Corp contract ($200K) -> Q3 revenue drop 15%. Positive: marketing enterprise campaign -> 40 leads -> $150K new contracts. The new contracts partially offset the Acme loss but Q4 revenue still below target. The two chains are independent — one is operational, one is marketing-driven.

questions.question: How did the dashboard prove the value of the earlier analysis?
questions.ground_truth: The dashboard (built because of the promotion earned from the analysis) caught a new packaging bottleneck before it caused delays, validating the monitoring approach that came from the original root cause work.

questions.question: How many hops in the causal chain connect the manager's Python suggestion to the packaging bottleneck being caught?
questions.ground_truth: At least 7 hops: manager suggested Python -> Udemy pandas course -> parsed sales CSVs -> discovered Q3 revenue drop -> root cause analysis presented -> promoted to data analyst -> built real-time dashboard -> dashboard caught packaging bottleneck. The connection is non-obvious because the suggestion to learn a programming language ultimately led to proactive operational monitoring.

questions.question: Trace the impact of the manager's original suggestion through to business outcomes.
questions.ground_truth: Manager suggested Python -> course -> data analysis -> discovered revenue problem -> root cause analysis -> 5 new hires -> promotion -> dashboard -> caught new bottleneck proactively.

questions.question: What two separate business problems were solved through the user's Python skills?
questions.ground_truth: 1) Identified root cause of Q3 revenue drop (warehouse staffing -> late deliveries -> lost Acme contract). 2) Built dashboard that caught packaging bottleneck before it caused delays.

questions.question: Trace the complete evidence chain that proves learning Python was the right decision.
questions.ground_truth: Python suggestion -> pandas course -> sales CSV analysis -> Q3 revenue drop discovery -> root cause analysis (staffing shortage) -> 5 new hires -> promotion to data analyst -> real-time dashboard -> caught packaging bottleneck proactively. Each step causally depends on the previous one.

questions.question: What is the single most causally important memory and why?
questions.ground_truth: The manager's suggestion to learn Python is the root cause of the entire chain. It's the most structurally important because removing it would collapse the entire downstream chain: no Python skills -> no data analysis -> no revenue problem discovery -> no root cause fix -> no promotion -> no dashboard -> no proactive bottleneck detection.

questions.question: Which causal chain — the operations fix or the marketing campaign — had more downstream impact?
questions.ground_truth: The operations chain (staffing fix) had far more downstream impact: it led to a promotion, a new role, a dashboard that catches future problems proactively, and ongoing organizational capability. The marketing campaign generated $150K in revenue but had no further downstream effects. The operations chain created lasting structural change.
